perturbation.original_text: Without the Company’s prior written consent, neither you nor any of your affiliates will for a period of two years from the date of this agreement directly or indirectly solicit for employment or employ any of the officers or employees of the Company (or any person whose activities are dedicated to the Company).
perturbation.changed_text: Without the Company’s prior written consent, neither you nor any of your affiliates will for a period of five years from the date of this agreement directly or indirectly solicit for employment or employ any of the officers or employees of the Company (or any person whose activities are dedicated to the Company).
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the non-solicitation period to five years is potentially unreasonable and could be deemed an unenforceable restraint of trade under California law, even though the contract is governed by Illinois law, the California law could be relevant if the employee lives in California.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements and restraints on trade. While this is a non-solicitation agreement, a five-year period could be considered an unreasonable restraint, especially if it significantly limits an employee's ability to find new employment, thus violating the spirit of Section 16600.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period from two to five years, which the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest could be an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' While the original text already presented a restraint, increasing it to five years exacerbates the potential violation of this law, making it a more significant and likely unenforceable restriction, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Return of Documents Upon the Company’s request, you shall promptly deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such materials, and shall certify the destruction of such materials in writing to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Return of Documents Upon the Company’s request, you shall deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such materials, and shall certify the destruction of such materials in writing to the Company.  This must be completed within 60 days of the company's request.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a 60-day deadline for returning or destroying confidential documents could conflict with the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) if the evaluation materials contained trade secrets that were misappropriated. The DTSA provides for a continuing cause of action for misappropriation, and imposing a lengthy delay could be seen as condoning continued use.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA creates a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. Allowing 60 days to return or destroy materials after a request arguably creates a loophole wherein misuse of trade secrets could continue for an extended period after notice.  The 'promptly' in the original clause is more aligned with protecting trade secret rights.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a specific 60-day timeframe for returning or destroying confidential documents, replacing the original requirement to do so 'promptly.' The Law Explanation posits that this delay could allow for continued misuse of trade secrets, which undermines the intent of the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) as it provides a cause of action for misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 USC 1836, outlines civil proceedings related to trade secret violations, confirming the legal basis for protecting trade secrets. The altered contract text, by allowing a 60-day window, weakens the immediate protection afforded by the DTSA, creating a potential loophole for ongoing misappropriation, which would violate the purpose of the DTSA. The contradiction is direct and impacts the level of protection given to trade secrets, making it a clear violation.

perturbation.original_text: You understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this agreement by you or your representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: You understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this agreement by you or your representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance as the only remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.explanation: Removing injunctive relief as a possible remedy limits the Company's options and might conflict with the DTSA which allows for injunctive relief to prevent the use or disclosure of misappropriated trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA specifically allows for injunctive relief in cases of trade secret misappropriation (18 U.S.C. § 1836(b)(3)(A)).  By limiting the remedy solely to specific performance, the agreement attempts to contractually waive a remedy explicitly provided for under federal law for trade secret misappropriation.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the option of injunctive relief for the Company, limiting it to only specific performance. The Law Explanation states that the DTSA allows for injunctive relief in trade secret misappropriation cases. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, confirms that the Attorney General may obtain injunctive relief against violations, and that an owner of a trade secret may bring a civil action. By limiting the remedy to only specific performance, the contract attempts to waive a remedy explicitly provided for under federal law, creating a legal contradiction as it weakens the legal requirement.
